questions.question: Explain the difference between 'su' and 'su -' commands with examples.
keyPoints: su switches user but preserves current shell environment
su - loads complete login environment of target user
su - changes to target user's home directory
Example: su - root loads root's PATH and HOME
Example: su root keeps current directory and environment

questions.question: What is the purpose of the -c option in su command? Give a practical example.
keyPoints: -c option executes a single command as target user
Does not start an interactive shell
Syntax: su -c 'command' username
Example: su -c 'ls -l /var/log' root
Useful for running specific admin commands

questions.question: Explain the security differences between 'su' and 'sudo' commands.
keyPoints: su requires target user's password (usually root)
sudo requires current user's password
sudo provides better logging and audit trails
sudo allows granular permission control
su gives full shell access; sudo can restrict commands

questions.question: What is the wheel group in Linux and how does it relate to su command?
keyPoints: wheel group is a special group for administrative users
Members of wheel group can use su to switch to root
Configured in /etc/pam.d/su with pam_wheel.so
Provides an extra layer of security
Only wheel members can su to root, others get 'Permission denied'

questions.question: How do you exit from an su session? What are the methods available?
keyPoints: Use 'exit' command to close the shell
Press Ctrl+D to send EOF and exit
Returns to original user account
All child processes are terminated on exit
Can also use 'logout' command in login shells

questions.question: What is PAM and how does it relate to su command authentication?
keyPoints: PAM stands for Pluggable Authentication Modules
Provides flexible authentication mechanism
Configuration file: /etc/pam.d/su
Controls who can use su and under what conditions
Can enforce group membership, lockout policies, etc.

questions.question: Why is it discouraged to run graphical applications as root using su?
keyPoints: Graphical apps may create files with root-only permissions
Can modify user configuration files incorrectly
Security risk if GUI application has vulnerabilities
Better to use 'pkexec' for GUI admin tasks
Creates confusion in file ownership

questions.question: Explain the su command in Linux with syntax, options, and practical examples. Include differences between various options.
keyPoints: su stands for Switch/Substitute User
Basic syntax: su [options] [-] [username]
Common options: -l (login), -c (command), -m (preserve env)
su - username: login shell with full environment
su username: non-login shell, keeps current environment
su -c 'command' username: execute single command
su -m: preserve user environment variables
Exit using 'exit' or Ctrl+D
Password required is target user's password
Security: avoid su to root over network connections

questions.question: Compare and contrast 'su' and 'sudo' commands in Linux. Include password requirements, security aspects, and use cases.
keyPoints: su requires target user's password (usually root)
sudo requires current user's password
sudo provides command-level granularity
sudo logs all commands to /var/log/auth.log or similar
su gives full shell access to target user
sudo can limit which commands a user can run
sudo is preferred for remote administration (SSH)
su is simpler for prolonged administrative sessions
sudo can timeout and require re-authentication
wheel group controls su access; sudo uses /etc/sudoers

questions.question: Explain the security considerations when using the su command in a Linux system. Discuss best practices.
keyPoints: Never use su to root over unencrypted network connections
Avoid leaving root shells idle or unattended
Use 'su -' for complete environment (not just 'su')
Monitor /var/log/ for su authentication attempts
Restrict su access using wheel group or PAM
Avoid running graphical apps as root
Use sudo for single commands instead of su
Set up proper authentication with strong passwords
Consider using 'sulog' or similar logging
Regularly audit who has su access to root

questions.question: Explain the role of PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) in su command authentication with relevant configuration files.
keyPoints: PAM provides modular authentication framework
Configuration file: /etc/pam.d/su
Controls authentication policy for su
Can enforce group membership requirements
pam_wheel.so: only allow wheel group to su root
Can set failed login attempts and lockout duration
pam_unix.so: standard UNIX authentication
pam_env.so: set environment variables
PAM allows flexible security policies
Misconfiguration can lock out all users
